user_query:
I saved a few family emails as .eml files that I want to sort for my scrapbook project and draft replies to family. Please work inside this workspace and produce the outputs under the output/ folder.

Inputs:
- EML files: input/messages/*.eml
- Keywords: input/keywords.txt (one keyword per line, for tagging messages)
- A broken helper script: scripts/extract_bodies.py

What I need:
1) Inspect the input/messages directory and create output/inspection.txt listing each .eml file name and its byte size (one file per line, name and size separated by a tab).

2) Run the provided script scripts/extract_bodies.py and capture all stdout/stderr into output/logs/first_run.log. Do not install any third‑party Python packages. Analyze the error message(s) in that log.
   - Perform a web search to find an official Python Software Foundation documentation page that explains parsing .eml emails using only the standard library (the "email" package). Use that to guide the fix. Record in output/research.txt the exact search query you used and the title of the documentation page you relied on (no URLs needed).

3) Fix or replace scripts/extract_bodies.py so that it uses only the Python standard library and successfully parses the EML messages. Process all files in input/messages and produce these artifacts:
   - For each message, write a normalized plain‑text body to output/bodies/<message-id>.txt (UTF‑8). If the message is multipart, prefer the text/plain part; correctly decode quoted‑printable or other common encodings.
   - Read keywords from input/keywords.txt and match them case‑insensitively as simple substring matches in the extracted body. Collect all matched keywords (unique, lowercased) for each message.
   - Create a CSV summary at output/summary.csv with header and exactly these columns per message: message_id, date_iso8601, from_name, from_email, subject, matched_keywords (semicolon‑separated), body_char_count. The date_iso8601 field must be the Date header as a full ISO‑8601 timestamp including offset, e.g., 2024-03-05T14:23:11-05:00.
   - For each message that matched at least one keyword, create a friendly reply draft at output/drafts/<message-id>_reply.txt that includes: (a) a first line "Subject: Re: <original subject>", (b) a second line "To: <from_email>", and (c) somewhere in the body the phrase "for our family scrapbook". Use the sender’s first name in a greeting if available from the From: display name.
   - Capture a successful processing run log at output/logs/second_run.log showing the command you executed and any summary output your script prints.

Constraints:
- Do not install or depend on any non‑stdlib packages; fix the script to use only Python’s standard library. Use a search engine to locate the relevant official docs from the Python Software Foundation and cite the doc title in output/research.txt.
- Treat every .eml in input/messages as an input; don’t skip files silently. If a file cannot be parsed, include a one‑line note in output/logs/second_run.log that names the file and the exception type.
- Keep all outputs under the output/ directory exactly as named above.

Deliverables to verify:
- output/inspection.txt
- output/logs/first_run.log
- output/research.txt
- output/bodies/<message-id>.txt for each message
- output/summary.csv with the specified columns
- output/drafts/<message-id>_reply.txt for messages with at least one keyword match
- output/logs/second_run.log All file paths are relative to the workspace directory.
